serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of spaghetti pomodoro is approximately 300 g, comprising cooked spaghetti topped with tomato and fresh basil sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Spaghetti with tomato sauce, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 20124 / FDC ID 169737)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked spaghetti with tomato sauce (FDC ID 169737). The dish is a close compositional match to spaghetti pomodoro (spaghetti, tomato, basil, olive oil). Minor adjustments applied to sodium to reflect a restaurant preparation. All core macros and secondary nutrients are within the expected range for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 45 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for spaghetti (white, boiled), which consistently reports GI values in the range 40–50 (mean ~45) due to the compact starch structure of pasta. Spaghetti pomodoro uses standard white spaghetti; the tomato-basil sauce contributes negligible additional glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (45 × 61.5 g available carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 = 27.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from tomato sauce and restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of spaghetti pomodoro provides approximately 630 mg sodium, representing about 27% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined pasta carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of ~27.7 per standard serving, spaghetti pomodoro produces a moderate postprandial glucose response; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g cooked and pair with a protein or vegetable side to blunt the glycemic effect.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and cholesterol-free profile with beneficial lycopene from tomato
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Spaghetti pomodoro is low in saturated fat (0.2 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, while the tomato sauce provides lycopene, an antioxidant associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; it is a heart-friendly pasta option when prepared with minimal added oil and salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spaghetti Pomodoro is a classic Italian pasta dish consisting of spaghetti — long, thin, cylindrical wheat-flour noodles — served with a simple tomato sauce made from ripe tomatoes, fresh basil, garlic, and olive oil. The dish is one of the foundational preparations of Italian cucina povera (peasant cooking), celebrated for its minimalism and reliance on high-quality ingredients. The spaghetti is boiled al dente, then tossed with the sauce so the pasta absorbs the flavour. The result is a dish with a tender yet slightly firm texture, a bright, mildly acidic tomato flavour, and a fragrant herbal note from fresh basil. Nutritionally, spaghetti pomodoro is a moderate-energy dish (~108 kcal per 100 g) dominated by complex carbohydrates (~20.5 g per 100 g) from the pasta, with modest protein (~3.4 g) and low fat (~1.5 g). It is naturally vegan and dairy-free. The glycemic index of white spaghetti is approximately 45 (low GI), owing to the compact starch matrix of extruded pasta, which slows digestion compared to bread or rice. The tomato sauce contributes lycopene, vitamin C, and potassium. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most dietary patterns but requires sodium monitoring in hypertensive individuals and portion control for those managing type 2 diabetes. In Singapore, it is commonly served at Italian restaurants and casual dining establishments, often as a vegetarian or vegan menu option.
common_user_queries: How many calories in spaghetti pomodoro?
What is the GI of spaghetti pomodoro?
Is spaghetti pomodoro low in fat?
How much sodium in spaghetti pomodoro?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked spaghetti with tomato sauce (FDC ID 169737), which is a close compositional match but not an exact match to the specific restaurant preparation at Ricciotti. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for white spaghetti (boiled), which is the standard pasta type used in this dish. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry exists for spaghetti pomodoro.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169737 (spaghetti with tomato sauce, cooked); this is the closest approved-source match. 2. Sodium value (210 mg per 100 g) reflects a moderate restaurant preparation; actual sodium may vary. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero per USDA source. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present in this dish; confirmed zero per USDA source. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as the dish uses olive oil and no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA source. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as the dish is fully plant-based and contains no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA source. 7. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for white spaghetti (boiled); gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 8. GL per serving = (45 × 61.5) / 100 = 27.7, where available carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 300 × 0.205 = 61.5 g. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169737 and are not sentinel values. 10. This dish is served at Ricciotti - The Riverwalk, Singapore (field survey source). 11. Cuisine tags 'Pizza' and 'Chicken Dish' from the input are not applicable to this specific dish and have been excluded from the output record.
